--- SOURCE 2: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X

SUMMARY:
Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase - ScienceDirect Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase Wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs) face significant challenges due to pH, temperature, and skin pollutants affecting glucose detection accuracy by disrupting D-glucose anomeric equilibrium. Updating the present level of research on the use of MUT in glucose sensors is critical for furthering the development of wearable glucose biosensors (WGB) designed for alternate bodily fluids such as sweat. This systematic review evaluates the advancements and prospects of mutarotase (MUT) in enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs).

FULL CONTENT:
Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase - ScienceDirect

Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase
=========================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Saminu Abdullahi a b, Zhu Yang a c, Mohamed Ishag Hassan Gama a b, Marwa Omer Mohammed Omer a b, Qilin Wang a, Abdulhadi Yakubu d, Zedong Nie a

Show more

Highlights
----------

*   •The systematic review examined the role of mutarotase in glucose detection. 
*   •Importance of addressing glucose anomeric properties for accurate monitoring. 
*   •Challenges and opportunities in integrating mutarotase into current glucose biosensors. 

Abstract
--------

Wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs) face significant challenges due to pH, temperature, and skin pollutants affecting glucose detection accuracy by disrupting D-glucose anomeric equilibrium. Although mutarotase (MUT) has historically addressed these challenges, recent research attention on MUT is limited. This systematic review evaluates the performance of biosensors utilizing MUT for enhanced glucose detection. Comprehensive searches across PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science identified 1,603 studies, of which 13 met PRISMA standards and were selected. Data were extracted and synthesized using pre-designed forms, with results presented through charts and tables. The reviewed studies did not provide clear data about the influence of MUT on the limit of detection (LOD). However, glucose biosensors incorporating MUT demonstrated sensitivity across a broad linear detection range, potentially eliminating the need for sample dilution in some instances. MUT also ensures a more accurate representation of total glucose levels in a sample, achieving complete glucose recovery (100 %) in 6 s in amperometric experiments and within 3-4 min in colorimetric, luminometric, polarimetric, and spectrophotometric studies. Despite stability concerns in 8 % of the studies, MUT proved effective across various pH (5.0–8.5) and temperature (20—37 ∘C) ranges. These results highlight the potential of MUT in advancing glucose wearable biosensing technology.

Wearable glucose biosensors

1. Introduction
---------------

Glucose biosensors have revolutionized diabetes management [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0001), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0003), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0004), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0005), historically relying on invasive techniques like finger-prick blood sampling [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0006), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0007). Recent advancements have shifted focus towards non-invasive technologies, particularly wearable glucose biosensors (WGB), aimed at improving user experience and accuracy in glucose monitoring [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0008), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0009), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0010), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0011). However, the widespread adoption of wearable biosensors faces formidable challenges. Environmental variability, including changes in pH, temperature, and humidity, poses significant obstacles to sensor accuracy and reliability [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0012), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0015). Overcoming these challenges requires robust sensor designs that can mitigate the effects of environmental fluctuations, ensuring consistent and precise measurements over time. Additionally, biofouling where biological substances accumulate on sensor surfaces presents a persistent issue that can degrade sensor performance, necessitating advanced materials and coatings to maintain sensor efficacy during prolonged use [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0016), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0017).

Biocompatibility is another critical concern, ensuring that wearable sensors are hypoallergenic and non-irritating for diverse user populations [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0018), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0019), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0020). Establishing reliable correlations between biomarkers measured in alternative body fluids (such as sweat or interstitial fluid) and blood levels is essential but requires rigorous validation across different demographics and health conditions. Power management emerges as a fundamental challenge, as wearable devices must balance energy efficiency with the need for sustained, uninterrupted monitoring [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0021), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0022), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0023). This necessitates innovative approaches to energy harvesting or storage to extend operational lifetimes and reduce reliance on frequent battery changes, thereby enhancing user convenience and compliance.

User acceptance and usability are pivotal for long-term adoption [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0024). Ensuring ergonomic designs that integrate seamlessly into daily life, coupled with lightweight, unobtrusive form factors, enhances user comfort and willingness to use these devices consistently. Moreover, navigating complex regulatory pathways and demonstrating adherence to stringent standards for accuracy, reliability, and safety adds substantial complexity and cost to sensor development and commercialization. Finally, achieving cost-effective manufacturing and scalability is crucial for broad accessibility, alongside improving sensor durability to withstand real-world conditions over extended periods [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0025). Addressing these multifaceted challenges through interdisciplinary collaboration and continuous innovation is paramount to realizing the full potential of wearable biosensors in personalized healthcare, ultimately improving patient outcomes worldwide.

Wearable Biosensors operate through a stepwise detection process that involves bio-recognition, transduction, and signal processing ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#fig0001)) [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0028). Initially, the bio-recognition element specifically interacts with the target analyte, such as an enzyme, antibody, or aptamer, generating an initial signal. This signal is then converted into a measurable form during the transduction step, which can utilize electrochemical, optical, or piezoelectric methods. The final step involves signal processing, where the transduced signal is amplified, filtered, and converted into a readable format, ultimately providing meaningful data to the user. In the development of these sensors, the first two detection steps, bio-recognition and transduction, receive most research attention. Researchers focus on enhancing the specificity and sensitivity of the bio-recognition elements, as well as improving the efficiency and accuracy of the transduction mechanisms.

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (317KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (317KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. General mechanism of glucose detection using enzymatic biosensors in the presence of mutarotase: Initially, the bio-recognition element (glucose oxidase enhanced with mutarotase) interacts with glucose, generating hydrogen peroxide **(Step 1)**. This signal is converted during the transduction step via (3) colorimetric/optical methods (oxidation of colored or light-emitting substrate) or (4) electrochemical methods (electron transfer) **(Step 2)**. The final step processes the signal by amplifying, filtering, and converting it into a readable format, providing meaningful data to the user **(Step 3)**. (Note: Reaction (3) may require peroxidase enzyme, depending on the substrate’s nature).

Depending on the transduction method, biosensors could generally be classified into electrochemical, optical or Piezoelectric [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0029), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0030), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0031), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0032). While the transduction step is also critical, the effectiveness and reliability of a biosensor start with the quality of the biorecognition step. A highly specific and sensitive biorecognition element ensures that the subsequent transduction step can accurately convert the interaction into a meaningful signal. For WGBs, the bio-recognition element typically involves enzymes, such as β-D-glucose oxidase (GOx) which catalyzes the oxidation of glucose, producing a detectable signal [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0033), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0034), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0035).

WGB has the ability to provide continuous, real-time physiological data by dynamically measuring glucose levels in biofluids like sweat and tears [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0036), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0037). Sweat has been the most readily accessible source of glucose, with the most sampling locations outside the body and ease of assortment device implantation [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0038), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0039). Sweat glucose and blood glucose are also clearly linked, making it perfect for wearable glucose monitoring [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0040), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0041), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0042). However, major obstacles remain in acquiring accurate sweat glucose data, notably the person-to-person variations due to environmental factors such as changes in pH and temperature, skin contamination [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0043), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0044), irregular sampling without iontophoretic stimulation, a low generation rate, and the mixing of old and new samples [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0045), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0046). More challenging is the low concentration of glucose found in sweat, which is estimated to be 100 times lower than in blood, necessitating the need for highly sensitive devices [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0047), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0048), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0049).

Enzyme-based glucose sensing has emerged as a promising technology in wearable glucose monitoring, offering several benefits in addition to its specificity [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0050), [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0051) and the need for a small sample volume [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0052), such as rapid response times, robustness, and cost-effectiveness [[53]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0053), [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0054). The enzyme (GOx) has an inherent capacity to withstand some alterations in environmental factors that include pH, ionic strength, and temperature [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0055), [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0056), [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0057), [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0058), as well as patient use conditions essential for reliability and clinical application of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0059), [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0060), [[61]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0061). However, its restricted specificity to the β-anomer of D-glucose may limit its application in WGB, particularly sweat glucose monitoring [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[63]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0063), [[64]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0064), [[65]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0065).

Metabolomic investigations indicate that α- and β-D-glucose anomers are present in biological samples at a relative percentage ratio estimated to be around 36-38 % and 62-64 % respectively [[66]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0066), [[67]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0067), [[68]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0068), [[69]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0069), [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0070), [[71]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0071), [[72]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0072), [[73]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0073). The equilibrium distribution of the anomers remains constant under steady physiological conditions [[74]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0074), [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0075) but can be altered by slight fluctuations in pH, temperature, or electrolytes [[76]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0076), [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0077), [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0078), [[79]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0079). When glucose samples are subjected to the GOx reaction, only the β-D-glucose anomer is oxidized [[80]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0080), [[81]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0081), [[82]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0082), [[83]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0083). The α-D-glucose anomer needs to be transformed into the β-anomer prior to oxidation [[84]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0084). Although this interconversion process may occur spontaneously, it generally requires time [[65]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0065), [[85]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0085), which might result in a longer response time or an underestimation of total glucose due to the incomplete conversion of α-D-glucose [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[86]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0086), [[87]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0087). Blood glucose levels can be reliably estimated using the β-anomer due to constant physiological parameters [[88]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0088). However, alternative body fluids such as sweat may have variations in the D-glucose anomeric proportion due to external differences in electrolyte composition, fluid pH, and temperature, which can affect the equilibrium distributions and cause accuracy concerns [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0077), [[89]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0089), [[90]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0090). This has prompted the need for a method to either maintain the anomeric equilibrium or entirely oxidize the total D-glucose anomers.

To accurately monitor glucose levels, β-D-glucose must be converted quickly and completely, as α-D-glucose cannot be oxidized by GOx [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0091). MUT has been shown to improve glucose measurement accuracy by ensuring complete glucose detection [[92]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0092). Nakashima et al., [[93]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0093) designed the peroxyoxalate method for measuring glucose in human serum, which used MUT to speed up the response time. Guemas et al., [[85]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0085) developed a GOx&HRP&Ferrocene electrode for amperometric detection of sucrose and glucose, demonstrating the effect of pre-addition of invertase and MUT to the sample solution, resulting in a response time of only 2 min. The majority of the MUT-based investigations were aimed at enhancing the accuracy, sensitivity, and response time of glucose biosensors [[89]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0089), [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0091), [[93]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0093), [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0094), [[95]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0095), [[96]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0096). Despite the enzyme’s promising potential, it has received less attention in recent research. This could be attributed to a lack of comprehensive research that investigates the enzyme’s complete range of functions while also recognizing potential obstacles.

Updating the present level of research on the use of MUT in glucose sensors is critical for furthering the development of wearable glucose biosensors (WGB) designed for alternate bodily fluids such as sweat. As a result, this review comprehensively assesses MUT’s potential for improving the sensitivity and accuracy of WGBs through signal amplification and accurate glucose detection, shedding light on its usefulness as a diabetes management tool. To the best of our knowledge, this kind of work is not available in existing literature. Therefore, by combining existing knowledge and identifying gaps in the literature, this study helps to shape the future trajectory of research in this vital area of healthcare technology.

2. Methods
----------

This systematic review evaluates the advancements and prospects of mutarotase (MUT) in enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs). The review was conducted by comprehensively s

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in wearable biosensors now enable the continuous monitoring of various health metrics, including ECGs and glucose levels, enhancing overall user health management.
Integration of technologies like mutarotase in glucose sensors significantly boosts sensitivity and accuracy, ensuring reliable readings even under varying environmental conditions.
User feedback indicates increased satisfaction with wearable biosensors due to real-time monitoring capabilities and ease of use, leading to higher engagement in health management.
The growing demand for personalized healthcare solutions has led to higher market adoption rates of wearable biosensors, driven by innovations in design and functionality.
